title: MLM: Multi-Layer Moire -- A Python Package for Generating Commensurate Supercells of Twisted Multilayer Two-Dimensional Materials
abstract: Moire superlattices formed by stacking atomically thin two-dimensional materials with a relative twist angle have emerged as a versatile platform for engineering quantum electronic, optical, and ferroic properties. Computational modelling of such systems with periodic boundary conditions requires the identification of commensurate supercells in which the moire periodicity is reproduced exactly, or within a prescribed tolerance. While several codes exist for bilayer systems, extension to three or more layers with independently chosen twist angles remains a significant challenge. Here we present MLM (Multi-Layer Moire), an open-source Python package that constructs periodic, PBC-compatible moire supercells for an arbitrary number of twisted layers with any Bravais lattice type. The package employs a solve-and-round algorithm that reduces the coincidence-site search to an $O(N^2)$ linear-algebra problem per twist angle, compared to the O(N^4) brute-force enumeration required by conventional approaches. We demonstrate the package on bilayer graphene, bilayer and trilayer MoS$_2$, bilayer SrTiO$_3$, and a PbTiO$_3$/SrTiO$_3$ oxide heterostructure, producing simulation-ready structure files for both VASP and LAMMPS. The fractional-coordinate atom-selection algorithm scales to supercells containing millions of atoms and is robust across all twist angles including very small angles below 1 degree.
summary: The MLM Python package provides an efficient way to generate periodic supercells for twisted multilayer 2D materials. By replacing brute-force searches with a linear-algebra-based approach, it significantly accelerates the discovery of commensurate structures, even for very small twist angles and complex multi-layer systems.
main_problem: Constructing periodic, commensurate supercells for twisted multilayer 2D materials is computationally expensive because conventional brute-force enumeration scales as O(N^4).
main_result: The authors developed MLM, a Python package that uses a 'solve-and-round' algorithm to reduce complexity to O(N^2), achieving speedup ratios of up to 216x and enabling the simulation of supercells with millions of atoms.
method: The 'solve-and-round' algorithm solves a 2x2 linear system to determine top-layer indices from bottom-layer vectors, combined with a sequential extension for multiple layers and a fractional-coordinate folding algorithm for atom selection.
model_or_system: The package is applied to twisted multilayer 2D materials with various Bravais lattices, including hexagonal (graphene, MoS2), square (SrTiO3), and mismatched heterostructures (PbTiO3/SrTiO3).
key_observables: Twist angle, mismatch residual (delta_vec), relative residual (rho), and supercell vector norm.
important_parameters: Twist angles (0 to 90 degrees), lattice constants, fractional tolerance (delta), and the integer index search window [n_min, n_max].
main_assumptions: The matching process is inherently asymmetric, treating the bottom layer as an exact reference while the top layer matches within a prescribed tolerance.
figures_summary: Visualizations of graphene and MoS2 supercells showing AA and AB stacking, a trilayer MoS2 supercell, and top-view images of SrTiO3 structures.
paper_structure: Introduction to the commensurability challenge, description of the MLM package and its solve-and-round algorithm, demonstration of the package on various 2D materials (graphene, MoS2, oxides), performance benchmarking against brute-force methods, and discussion of limitations and future work.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Top view of the 16.43◦commensurate bilayer graphene supercell . Carbon atoms from the bottom and top layer are shown in cyan. The parallelogram outlines the moiré unit cell defined by the two supercell vectors Asc 1 and Asc 2 .
Figure 2: Top view of the 17.89◦commensurate bilayer MoS2 supercell . Mo atoms are shown in teal, S atoms in yellow.
Figure 3: Top view of a trilayer MoS2 commensurate supercell with θ1 = 27.8◦between layers 1 and 2, and θ2 = 40.97◦between layers 1 and 3.
Figure 4: Top view of a commensurate bilayer SrTiO3 supercell at a representative twist angle of 16.26◦. Sr, Ti, and O atoms are shown in green, blue, and cyan respectively.
Figure 5: Top view of an approximately commensurate PbTiO3/SrTiO3 heterostructure supercell at twist of 7.13◦
